narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average, along with your LSAT score of 31, family in the upper-middle income bracket, and plans to attend full-time—yet your application was rejected based on a model's assessment of your bar exam prospects. The model predicts a 48% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough for the university to deny admission, as they prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record from earlier years, which carried the most weight in the decision. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1st—well below the average 6th decile—stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure, suggesting early struggles that signal potential challenges ahead. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 2nd, also lower than the typical 6th decile, reinforced this concern, acting as the second-biggest influence toward a failure prediction. On a brighter note, your solid undergraduate GPA was the top positive factor, pulling the prediction back toward success and showing your ability to perform well in college-level work. Compared to typical students, your early decile rankings are in the bottom tier, which unusually outweighed your stronger later GPA for this model. While your LSAT and family background played roles, they weren't the deciding ones here. This doesn't define your potential—many students overcome early hurdles to excel. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 37 average, strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your growth as a Hispanic student committed to law. Reapplying next cycle with these improvements could shift the model's view in your favor.
